_doc: Canonical default projects per PMF step. Loaded into dashboard_data.data.projects at onboarding (for start step) and at phase-change (additive for new step). Personalized by the agent based on business_profile.industry / .buyer / .product_description. Mirrors kpis_per_step.json structure. Each step has 5–7 projects designed to (a) advance the step's mainGoal and (b) work against the canonical bottlenecks in roadmap_defaults.json. Each project also carries 2–3 anchor `tasks` (deterministic, structural — the agent appends 1–2 user-tailored tasks at write time based on business_profile + step_debrief). At write time the agent adds `n` (1-indexed), `status: 'later'` (or 'current' for the obviously first project), and `step: '<sub_id>'`.

projects.title: Run 15 problem interviews with engaged-user candidates
projects.outcome: Documented notes from 15 interviews with cold-outreached prospects (not friends)
projects.goal: 5 verbatim quotes that pass the Mom Test
projects.why: Engaged-user sample is the only way to find real pain — friends and family produce politeness yeses
tasks: Draft an interview guide with 7 past-behavior questions (no hypotheticals)
Build a candidate list of 30 cold prospects matching the engaged-user profile
Log every interview in a single shared doc with date, name, and 3 key quotes

projects.title: Document 5 verbatim user quotes proving the problem exists
projects.outcome: 5 dated quotes with name and source in a single document, ready to share
projects.goal: Convert evidence in your head into shareable proof
projects.why: Quotes in your head don't count — written evidence is what convinces you, investors and future hires
tasks: Create a quotes doc with columns: quote, name, role, date, source interview
Review existing interview notes and extract every direct pain-quote
Filter to the 5 strongest quotes naming pain, frequency, and workaround

projects.title: Master the Mom Test interview style
projects.outcome: A 1-page cheat sheet of allowed and forbidden questions above your desk
projects.goal: Stop leading questions from producing fake validation
projects.why: Leading questions ("would X be useful?") produce noise — only past-behavior questions surface real pain
tasks: Read or re-read The Mom Test (focus on chapters 1–3) and take 1 page of notes
Write a 1-page cheat sheet: 5 allowed questions, 5 forbidden questions
Record one practice interview and audit it against the cheat sheet

projects.title: Write your founder-market-fit statement
projects.outcome: One paragraph answering why YOU, why THIS market, for the next 10 years
projects.goal: A 1-sentence statement you can say without hesitation
projects.why: Without a clear personal answer you will quit when the problem gets hard — which it will
tasks: Brainstorm 3 personal reasons why this market matters to you for 10 years
Draft a 1-paragraph founder-market-fit statement and read it out loud

projects.title: Build an engaged-user pipeline via cold outreach
projects.outcome: 30+ LinkedIn or community connections of people actively dealing with the problem
projects.goal: A pipeline that produces 5 new interviews per week
projects.why: Pipeline is the difference between 15 real interviews and 5 polite chats with friends
tasks: Write a 3-line outreach message that names the problem and asks for 20 minutes
Build a tracking sheet: name, channel, sent date, replied, booked, completed
Send 10 cold outreach messages per day for 2 weeks and log every reply

projects.title: Find and study 3 existing workarounds users have built
projects.outcome: Documentation of the spreadsheets, scripts and manual hacks users use today
projects.goal: Proof that pain is real and someone is paying to avoid it
projects.why: Existing workarounds are the strongest signal that pain is real and willingness-to-pay exists
tasks: Ask every interviewee: "show me what you use today to solve this"
Capture 3 user-built workarounds as screenshots (sheet, script, or hack)
Write a 1-page summary: what each workaround does, costs, and fails at

projects.title: Define MVP scope in 1 page (3–5 core features)
projects.outcome: A scope doc you could hand to a stranger to build
projects.goal: A locked scope before you write a line of code
projects.why: Scope creep is the #1 reason MVPs ship late — write it down before you build
tasks: List every feature idea and rank by user-pain evidence from 1.1 interviews
Cut the list to 3–5 features that solve the single sharpest pain
Write the 1-page scope doc and circulate to 1 trusted advisor for a sanity check

projects.title: Ship a fake-it MVP using no-code (Zapier / Airtable / Sheets)
projects.outcome: A live URL or shared workspace users can interact with
projects.goal: Users in the product within 2 weeks
projects.why: Speed-to-market beats code quality — every week of building without users is a week of guessing
tasks: Pick the no-code stack (Airtable / Zapier / Softr) and set up the workspace
Build the happy-path flow for the #1 use case (skip edge cases for now)
Send the live link to 1 friendly user and watch them complete the flow

projects.title: Recruit 5 founding users with cold-outreach scripts
projects.outcome: 5 users who agreed in writing to try the MVP and give weekly feedback
projects.goal: A user count, not a sign-up count
projects.why: 5 committed users beat 50 sign-ups who never log in
tasks: Draft a founding-user pitch: what they get, what you ask (8 weeks of feedback)
Reach out to 20 candidates and track yes/no/maybe in a sheet
Get written commitment (email or DM) from 5 users before granting access

projects.title: Set up a Slack or email channel with founding users
projects.outcome: A shared space where users report friction in real time
projects.goal: Continuous feedback, not scheduled feedback bursts
projects.why: User feedback should flow daily — weekly cadence is already too slow this early
tasks: Create the channel (Slack / Discord / email thread) and invite the 5 users
Post a kickoff message naming the cadence: report any friction same-day
Respond to every message within 2 hours for the first 2 weeks

projects.title: Instrument basic usage tracking (PostHog / Plausible / Sheet)
projects.outcome: A dashboard showing sessions, feature usage, drop-off per user
projects.goal: Numbers, not narrative
projects.why: Without numbers you'll bias toward the user who complained loudest, not the data
tasks: Pick a tracking tool (PostHog free / Plausible / a simple sheet) and install it
Define and instrument 3 key events: signup, first-action, return-visit
Build a 1-page dashboard reviewed every Friday

projects.title: Run a post-launch friction debrief after 2 weeks
projects.outcome: A 1-page report listing what users actually tried and where they dropped off
projects.goal: Turn launch into a learning machine
projects.why: The post-launch debrief is what turns the MVP from a build into a learning machine
tasks: Schedule a 30-min retro on day 14 post-launch with the founding-user data ready
Cross-reference usage data with channel feedback for the top 3 friction points
Write the 1-page debrief: what worked, what broke, what you'll change in week 3

projects.title: Run observation sessions with 10 users over 2 weeks
projects.outcome: Session notes for 20 sessions (2 per user) — what they tried, where they stuck
projects.goal: Behavior data, not interview data
projects.why: Observation beats interviews — users say one thing and do another
tasks: Build a session-note template: timestamp, action, friction, quote, screen
Schedule 20 sessions (2 per user) across a 2-week window
Run each session screen-shared, stay silent, take notes — never lead the user

projects.title: Map 3 core friction patterns from observation notes
projects.outcome: A document naming each pattern with the quote and screen evidence
projects.goal: 3 patterns, not 30 issues
projects.why: Patterns turn 20 sessions of noise into 3 actionable signals
tasks: Re-read all 20 session notes and tag each friction with a 1-word category
Group categories into 3 dominant patterns with 4+ supporting observations each
Document each pattern: name, 1 quote, 1 screenshot, recommended next step

projects.title: Build a weekly engagement tracker (Airtable / Sheets)
projects.outcome: A grid showing sessions, key actions, churn signals per user per week
projects.goal: Leading indicators, weekly
projects.why: Engagement is the leading indicator of PMF — track it before churn shows up
tasks: List the 3 key actions that signal engagement for your product
Build a sheet with 1 row per user, 1 column per week, cells for action counts
Set a Friday 30-min review block to update the grid and flag drops

projects.title: Create user archetype v1 from observation
projects.outcome: A 1-page description of who your engaged users are and what's sticky
projects.goal: A sharp profile of who you build for
projects.why: Without an archetype you optimize for the loudest voice, not the most valuable one
tasks: Rank the 10 users by engagement and identify the top 3 most-engaged
List what the top 3 have in common: role, company size, trigger, frequency
Write the 1-page archetype doc and pin it visible to the team

projects.title: Run weekly founder office hours with all 10 users
projects.outcome: A 30-min weekly sync that keeps users engaged and surfaces new friction
projects.goal: High-touch retention this stage
projects.why: Founder-touch is the cheapest retention tool you have at this stage
tasks: Pick a recurring 30-min weekly slot and send calendar invites to all 10 users
Build a 3-question agenda: what worked, what broke, what's missing
Post a 3-line recap after every session in the shared channel

projects.title: Prioritize top 3 product hypotheses for the next 30 days
projects.outcome: A backlog of 3 ranked hypotheses with user-evidence for each
projects.goal: Focus by exclusion
projects.why: A backlog of 3 forces you NOT to build the next ten random ideas
tasks: List every product idea raised in the last 30 days from any source
Score each idea by user-evidence count and impact-on-engagement
Pick the top 3, write the hypothesis for each, archive the rest in a parking lot

projects.title: Run Sean Ellis PMF survey with all engaged users
projects.outcome: Raw survey results with V/S/N split and calculated %Very Disappointed
projects.goal: A number, not a vibe
projects.why: Sean Ellis is the canonical PMF question — without it you're guessing
tasks: Set up the Sean Ellis survey (Typeform / Form) with the canonical 4 questions
Send to every user who has used the product in the last 2 weeks
Calculate %Very Disappointed and log raw V/S/N counts in a shared doc

projects.title: Build a V/S/N segmentation matrix
projects.outcome: A pivot showing which user archetypes give V vs S vs N
projects.goal: Per-segment PMF, not a global average
projects.why: One global PMF score hides which segment you should double down on
tasks: Tag each respondent with their archetype, role, and primary use case
Build a pivot: rows = segment, columns = V/S/N counts and % Very Disappointed
Identify the segment with highest % Very Disappointed and write a 3-line memo

projects.title: Interview 3 Very Disappointed users in depth
projects.outcome: Documented reasons why these users would miss your product
projects.goal: Articulate what they cling to
projects.why: The V segment IS your product — understand what they cling to before you change anything
tasks: Pick 3 Very Disappointed users with different archetypes if possible
Run 45-min interviews on: what would you replace it with, what would you lose
Document the 3 answers verbatim plus what each user would miss most

projects.title: Define the favorite-user persona with 5 mandatory fields
projects.outcome: Persona doc: role, problem solved, usage frequency, growth trajectory, willingness to pay
projects.goal: A single target for marketing, sales and product
projects.why: Without a sharp persona, marketing, sales and product all aim at different targets
tasks: Pull the 5 fields from your V-segment data and find the modal value for each
Write the persona doc as a single named character (e.g. "Maria, Head of Ops")
Share the persona with 1 customer-facing person and have them stress-test it

projects.title: Benchmark your score against the 40% PMF bar
projects.outcome: A written analysis: where you are, what the gap is, what closes it
projects.goal: Honest distance to PMF
projects.why: Benchmarks anchor expectations — 25% is honest progress, not failure
tasks: Compute the gap: current % Very Disappointed minus 40%
List 3 specific actions (feature, segment, message) that could close the gap
Write a 1-page honest assessment naming where you are and what's next

projects.title: Document 3 fanatic patterns from V-segment users
projects.outcome: Notes on what V-users do, say and feel that other users don't
projects.goal: Inputs for the 1.5 doubling-down phase
projects.why: Fanatic patterns become the 1.5 doubling-down targets — capture them now
tasks: Re-read the 3 V-segment interviews and tag every behavior unique to V-users
Group behaviors into 3 patterns with at least 2 supporting examples each
Document each pattern with 1 quote and a candidate doubling-down move

projects.title: Double down on your #1 fanatic pattern with 3 feature builds
projects.outcome: 3 shipped improvements that deepen what V-users already love
projects.goal: Widen the moat for the segment you already won
projects.why: Doubling-down extends your moat — features Hopeful users want are not moats yet
tasks: Pick the #1 fanatic pattern from 1.4 and brainstorm 5 ways to deepen it
Scope the top 3 as 1–2 week shippable units with clear acceptance criteria
Ship one per 2-week cycle and track V-user engagement on the relevant action

projects.title: Run validation interviews after each ship (5 users per feature)
projects.outcome: 15 interviews documenting whether the feature actually deepens love
projects.goal: Shipped + validated, not shipped
projects.why: Shipping without measurement turns 1.5 into feature-zoo theatre
tasks: Build a 5-question script around "would you miss this if it disappeared"
Run 5 interviews within 14 days of each ship and log answers in a shared doc
Summarize each cohort: % who notice, % who care, % who would miss it

projects.title: Identify and unblock 3 Hopeful users monthly
projects.outcome: 3 interventions (feature, process or pivot) to convert Hopeful → Very Disappointed
projects.goal: Convert the cheapest tier of upside
projects.why: Hopeful users are the second-cheapest segment to convert if you fix their blocker
tasks: Filter survey results for the "somewhat disappointed" segment and pick 3 users
Interview each about the single blocker that prevents them from becoming V
Ship one targeted intervention per user and re-survey 30 days later

projects.title: Measure Sean Ellis monthly (2 consecutive months ≥40%)
projects.outcome: 2 monthly survey results showing upward or stable trajectory ≥40%
projects.goal: Two ≥40% in a row, not one lucky reading
projects.why: One survey ≥40% is luck — two consecutive proves PMF
tasks: Set a recurring monthly calendar block for survey send + analysis
Send the survey on the same day each month to all active users
Plot the score trendline in a shared sheet and review with the team monthly

projects.title: Document weekly 50/50 doubling-down vs unblocking decisions
projects.outcome: A weekly log of which users and features got focus and why
projects.goal: Explicit allocation, not drift
projects.why: Without explicit splitting you'll drift to one side and stall the other
tasks: Create a weekly allocation log: 1 column for doubling-down, 1 for unblocking
Every Friday log what each side actually consumed in hours and what shipped
Review monthly and rebalance if one side drifted below 30% of total effort

projects.title: Run a cohort retention analysis (8-week window)
projects.outcome: A grid showing % of month-1 users still active in weeks 5–8
projects.goal: Retention as PMF confirmation
projects.why: Retention is the lagging proof of PMF — Sean Ellis is the leading proof
tasks: Pull a list of users who signed up 8+ weeks ago and tag their signup-week cohort
Build the retention grid: rows = cohort, columns = wks 1–8, cell = % active
Write a 1-page read-out naming flat-line week (the start of true retention)
